← Back to SummariesFull text ↗

Parameter Optimization and Larger Precision for (T)FHE

IACR ePrint 2022/704 (Journal of Cryptology, 2023) · full view ↗

At a glance

Authors: Loris Bergerat, Anas Boudi, Quentin Bourgerie, Ilaria Chillotti, Damien Ligier, Jean-Baptiste Orfila, Samuel Tap, all at Zama (Paris, France). Full version on IACR ePrint 2022/704; published in the Journal of Cryptology (2023). 73 pages including supplementary material.

One sentence: The paper attacks the practical bottleneck of FHE - choosing cryptographic parameters that are simultaneously secure, correct, and fast - by formalizing parameter selection as a constrained optimization problem with per-operator noise and cost models, and pairs this with new large-precision machinery (a padding-free WoP-PBS and a hybrid radix/CRT integer encoding) so that TFHE can compute on 16-bit and 32-bit integers efficiently.

Two intertwined contributions:

  • An optimization framework that takes a crypto-free arithmetic graph plus a target security level and failure probability, and emits an FHE graph together with its optimal parameter set. It is the first such tool for TFHE-like (bootstrapping-heavy, multi-precision) schemes.
  • Larger-precision constructions: a generalized radix/CRT/hybrid multi-ciphertext encoding of large integers, two new homomorphic modular-reduction algorithms for arbitrary modulus in the radix approach, and a new WoP-PBS (programmable bootstrap that needs no bit of padding) that evaluates a generic LUT over a multi-ciphertext integer.

Headline findings:

  • The classical TFHE PBS only scales to about 5 bits of message precision before becoming impractical (each extra bit roughly doubles the cost above 4 to 5 bits, because the polynomial size $N$ must double), and no feasible parameters exist beyond $\approx 11$ bits.
  • For larger precision it is far better to split a message across several small ciphertexts than to inflate parameters for one. The new WoP-PBS scales roughly linearly (not exponentially) in message size for generic LUTs, up to about 24 bits.
  • Concrete benchmarks (TFHE-rs, single Intel Xeon Platinum 8375C, 128-bit security): a generic 16-bit LUT in $\approx 0.76$ to $1.80\,\text{s}$; 16-bit homomorphic multiply in $\approx 0.40\,\text{s}$ (CRT, sequential) to $\approx 5.73$ to $29\,\text{s}$ (radix); 32-bit hybrid multiply $\approx 1.82\,\text{s}$ on 4 threads.
  • Security is enforced via the lattice-estimator [APS15] used as a security oracle; correctness via Gaussian-tail failure-probability bounds; the whole search reduces to optimizing a single worst-case atomic pattern, solvable in well under a second.
  • All parameters are produced by the framework and all benchmarks run on TFHE-rs, Zama's successor to the Concrete library [CJL+20], so the techniques directly underpin Zama's integer/Concrete stack.

The problem: secure AND correct AND fast, all at once

In theory FHE computes anything over ciphertexts; in practice the obstacle is parameter selection. Every TFHE-like computation must fix many knobs, which the authors split into macro-parameters (the LWE dimension $n$, the GLWE dimension $k$, the polynomial size $N$, the ciphertext modulus $q$, and the noise levels) and micro-parameters (per-operator decomposition base $\beta$ and number of levels $\ell$ for a key-switch or a bootstrap; these are local to one FHE operator). These must jointly satisfy three guarantees:

  • Security: the (R)LWE instance must be hard. The product $k\cdot N$ (or $n$ for LWE), the secret-key distribution, the noise, and $q$ together determine the level; the LWE/lattice-estimator [APS15] judges it. Security is easy in the sense that one can always add encryption noise to reach the target, so the search reduces to picking dimensions and then taking the minimal secure noise.
  • Correctness: noise grows through each operator; if it crosses a threshold, decryption returns the wrong message. One must track noise via per-operator noise formulae and keep it below a bound at every edge of the circuit.
  • Cost: among all correct, secure choices, minimize a cost model (here, single-thread running time approximated by operation counts).

The central question of Section 3 is: for a given graph of FHE operators, how to find parameters so the evaluation is the fastest while preserving both correctness and security? The authors note this was an open problem before their contribution. Existing FHE compilers either target batched, bootstrap-free schemes (BGV/B/FV/HEAAN, where the lever is multiplicative-depth reduction [CAS17, ACS20, LLOY20]) or use hard-coded parameters for Boolean TFHE gate-bootstrapping circuits [CMG+18, CDS15]. To the authors' knowledge no prior work optimized parameters for an FHE scheme that includes a bootstrap and supports multi-precision plaintexts.

Encoding, ciphertexts, and the failure criterion

The plaintext encoding fixes a ciphertext modulus $q$, a message modulus $p$, and $\pi$ padding bits with $2^{\pi}\cdot p \le q$. A message $M$ is encoded as $\widetilde{M} = \lfloor \Delta\cdot M \rceil$ with the scaling factor $\Delta = \frac{q}{2^{\pi}\cdot p}$, and decoded by $\lfloor \widetilde{M}/\Delta \rceil \in \mathbb{Z}_{2^{\pi}\cdot p}$. In practice the encoded value carries a small error polynomial $E=\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} e_i X^i$, so $\widetilde{M}=\Delta\cdot M + E$, and decoding fails as soon as some coefficient satisfies $|e_i|\ge \Delta/2$:

$$P\!\left(\bigcup_i \left\{|e_i|\ge \tfrac{\Delta}{2}\right\}\right) = P\!\left(\mathrm{Decode}(\widetilde{M},\,2^{\pi}\cdot p,\,q)\neq M\right)$$

Equation 1 is the quantity the whole correctness analysis bounds. A GLWE ciphertext of $\widetilde{M}$ under secret key $\vec{S}=(S_1,\dots,S_k)\in\mathcal{R}_q^k$ is the tuple $\left(A_1,\dots,A_k,\,B=\sum_{i=1}^{k}A_i\cdot S_i+\widetilde{M}+E\right)$ with the $A_i$ uniform in $\mathcal{R}_q=\mathbb{Z}_q[X]/(X^N+1)$ (with $N$ a power of two) and $E$ Gaussian. Setting $N=1$ gives an LWE ciphertext (then the key size is written $n=k$); setting $k=1,\,N\gt 1$ gives an RLWE ciphertext. A GGSW ciphertext is a redundant stack of $(k+1)\ell$ GLWE ciphertexts parameterized by a decomposition base $\beta$ and a number of levels $\ell$; GGSW ciphertexts are used for bootstrapping keys and in circuit bootstrapping.

The three TFHE workhorses are: the key switch (KS), which changes the secret key (and a few parameters) at the price of added noise, signature $\mathrm{ct}_{\mathrm{out}}\leftarrow\mathrm{KS}(\mathrm{ct}_{\mathrm{in}},\mathrm{KSK})$; the programmable bootstrap (PBS), a modulus switch (MS) then blind rotation (BR) then sample extraction (SE) that simultaneously reduces noise and applies a LUT $L$, signature $\mathrm{ct}_{\mathrm{out}}\leftarrow\mathrm{PBS}(\mathrm{ct}_{\mathrm{in}},\mathrm{BSK},P_L)$; and the homomorphic dot product (DP). When a KS feeds a PBS the pair is written $\mathrm{KS\text{-}PBS}(\mathrm{ct}_{\mathrm{in}},\mathrm{PUB},P_f)$ with $\mathrm{PUB}=(\mathrm{BSK},\mathrm{KSK})$. The PBS has a small probability of returning $L[m+\epsilon]$ with $\epsilon\neq 0$. The generalized PBS of [CLOT21] adds two parameters $(\kappa,\vartheta)$ controlling exactly which part of the plaintext the modulus switch extracts. The circuit bootstrapping operator [CGGI20] turns an LWE ciphertext into a GGSW ciphertext (a PBS followed by an LWE-to-GLWE functional KS), and horizontal / vertical / mixed packing evaluate LUTs in a leveled way via CMux gates, taking the input bits as a list of GGSW ciphertexts.

Failure probability: from Gaussian tails to a noise bound

Correctness is made quantitative through the standard score (Definition 1). For $A\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ and the error function $\mathrm{erf}(z)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_0^z e^{-t^2}\,dt$,

$$z^{*}(p_{\mathrm{fail}}) = \sqrt{2}\cdot \mathrm{erf}^{-1}\!\left(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}}\right), \qquad P\bigl(A\notin\,]\!-\!z^{*}\sigma,\,z^{*}\sigma[\,\bigr)\le p_{\mathrm{fail}}$$

Equation 2 says a centered Gaussian of standard deviation $\sigma$ lands outside $(-z^{*}\sigma,\,z^{*}\sigma)$ with probability at most $p_{\mathrm{fail}}$, and more generally $z^{*}(p_{\mathrm{fail}})\cdot\sigma\le t \Rightarrow P(A\notin\,]\!-\!t,t[)\le p_{\mathrm{fail}}$. Since decoding fails exactly when a coefficient exceeds $\Delta/2$, the authors derive (Definition 8, Remark 2) an explicit noise bound:

$$t_{\alpha}(\pi,p) = \frac{\Delta}{2\cdot z^{*}(p_{\mathrm{fail}})}, \qquad p_{\mathrm{fail}} = 1-\sqrt[N]{1-\alpha}$$

Equation 3 guarantees correct decoding with probability $\ge 1-\alpha$ whenever $\sigma \le t_{\alpha}$. The $N$-th root comes from a union bound over the $N$ independent coefficients (for an LWE ciphertext $N=1$, so $p_{\mathrm{fail}}=\alpha$). The tightness of the noise model is what makes the confidence interval, and hence the chosen parameters, trustworthy.

This turns correctness into a clean set membership. With $\{\sigma_i\}_{i\in I}$ the noise standard deviations on every edge of the FHE DAG $\mathcal{G}=(V,L)$, the per-edge feasible set is $\mathcal{S}_i=\{x\in\mathcal{P}\mid \sigma_i(x)\le t_{\alpha}(p_i)\}$, and the noise feasible set (Definition 9) is their intersection

$$\mathcal{S} = \bigcap_{i\in I}\mathcal{S}_i = \left\{\, x\in\mathcal{P} \;\middle|\; \forall i\in[\![1,|L|]\!],\ \sigma_i(x)\le t_{\alpha}(p_i) \,\right\}$$

Equation 4 collects all parameter choices that yield a correct computation, drawn from the search space $\mathcal{P}$ (the Cartesian product of every micro- and macro-parameter search space, e.g. $\mathcal{P}_N=\{2^8,\dots,2^{17}\}$, $\mathcal{P}_n=[\![256,2048]\!]$, $\mathcal{P}_k=[\![1,6]\!]$, and $(\log_2\beta,\ell)$ treated as a single variable subject to $\ell\cdot\log_2\beta\le\log_2 q$). The FHE optimization problem is then

$$\arg\min_{x\in\mathcal{P}}\ \mathrm{Cost}(\mathcal{G},x) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad x\in\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G})$$

Equation 5 is a constrained minimization that classical solvers (branch-and-bound) can attack. The remaining difficulty is that $\mathcal{S}$ depends on the whole graph, which can be huge; the next sections simplify it. (Remark 3 notes the same machinery admits other feasible sets, e.g. bounds on public-key size or bandwidth.)

The noise and cost model, and the security oracle

Each FHE operator (Definitions 4 to 5) is equipped with three things: a plain operator (the cleartext function it must reproduce), a noise model (a formula giving the output noise variance from the input variances, the cryptographic parameters, and the plaintext values), and a cost model (a surrogate for the metric to minimize - time, power, or price). Noise is always a variance of a centered Gaussian; more noise means larger variance.

The security oracle (Definition 6) is built from the lattice-estimator: given $k\cdot N$, a security level $\lambda$ and modulus $q$, it returns the minimal noise variance $\sigma_{\min}^2$ needed for security. This decouples security from the optimization: once dimensions are chosen, the encryption noise is pinned to the oracle's value, so the optimizer never wastes effort searching noise. As a rule of thumb, increasing $k\cdot N$ lets one decrease the minimal noise at fixed security.

The cost model counts the algorithmic work of each operator (additions, multiplications, integer casts, and the asymptotic FFT cost) as a proxy for single-thread time. For instance the polynomial dot product $\sum_{i=1}^{\alpha} M_i\cdot CT_i$ (with $M_i\in\mathcal{R}_q$ polynomials and $CT_i=(A_i,B_i)\in\mathcal{R}_q^2$ RLWE ciphertexts) costs

$$\underbrace{(2+1)\,\alpha\, N\log N}_{\text{to FFT domain}} + \underbrace{2\alpha N}_{\text{float }\times} + \underbrace{(\alpha-1)N}_{\text{float }+} + \underbrace{2N\log N}_{\text{to standard domain}}$$

Equation 6 treats a float multiply as costing the same as an integer add, which is close enough in practice to yield efficient parameters. The cost of the dot product itself is taken negligible, and the cost of an atomic pattern is the sum of the costs of the FHE operators inside it (essentially the PBS plus the KS). A subtle but important detail: because the TFHE PBS uses an FFT in $f64$, the authors add an empirical FFT-noise correction to the bootstrap output variance,

$$n\cdot 2^{\omega_1}\cdot \ell\cdot \beta^2\cdot N^2\cdot (k+1), \qquad \omega_1 \approx 22 - 2.6$$

Equation 7 captures mantissa-bit loss: the $22 = 2\cdot(64-53)$ term comes from the 53-bit $f64$ mantissa against $q=2^{64}$, and $2.6$ is an experimental fit. Casting the bootstrapping key from 64-bit integers to floats loses LSBs, and error accumulates in the Fourier domain; collecting data over many parameter settings yields this corrective term.

Shrinking the search: atomic patterns, domination, and Pareto fronts

The key simplification (Section 3.2) is to chop the graph at the operators whose output noise is independent of their input noise. Definition 10 splits operators into category (i), which output noise independent of the input (the PBS), and category (ii), which add to the input noise (the KS or the dot product). Between two consecutive category-(i) operators lies an atomic pattern (AP) (Definition 11): a sub-graph whose noise can be analyzed in isolation. The canonical AP type $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ is a dot product, then a key switch, then a PBS, and it is fully characterized by two numbers: the dot-product 2-norm $\nu$ and the noise bound $t$, written $A=A(\nu,t)$. The 2-norm controls how the DP scales input noise: for weights $\{\omega_i\}_{i\in I}$ and inputs with noise $\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$, the output variance is $\nu_2^2\,\sigma^2$ with

$$\nu_2^2 = \sum_{i\in I}\omega_i^2$$

Equation 8 is the squared 2-norm of the integer weights. This admits a domination relation (Definition 12, Theorem 1): if $\nu_1\le\nu_2$ and $t_2\le t_1$ then $\mathcal{S}(A_2(\nu_2,t_2))\subset\mathcal{S}(A_1(\nu_1,t_1))$, so the harder AP dominates the easier one; satisfying its constraint satisfies the other's. Therefore one only needs the non-dominated APs, that is the Pareto front $\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{pareto}}=\mathrm{Pareto}(\mathcal{G})$, and $\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G})=\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{pareto}})$. Because the dot-product cost is taken negligible, the cost of an AP of this type is independent of the particular instance ($\mathrm{Cost}(A_i,x)=\mathrm{Cost}(A_j,x)$), so minimizing the whole-graph cost reduces to minimizing one atomic pattern:

$$\arg\min_{x\in\mathcal{P}}\ \mathrm{Cost}(\cdot,x) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad x\in\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{pareto}})$$

Equation 9 is the simplified problem actually solved. Two bracketing graphs give bounds on the optimum: $\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{worst}}=\{A(\max_i\nu_i,\ \min_i t_i)\}$ has $\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{worst}})\subset\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G})$ so it yields a feasible (upper-bound) solution, while $\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{best}}=\{A(\nu^{*},t^{*})\}$ with $t^{*}=\min_i t_i$ gives a lower bound; they coincide (optimal) when $\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{pareto}}=\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{worst}}=\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{best}}$, i.e. a uniform graph. A further shortcut: in $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ the noise increases monotonically until the modulus switch inside the final PBS, so the bound need only be checked at that one point, $\mathcal{S}(A_1)=\{x\mid\sigma_{\mathrm{MS},1}(x)\le t\}$. This machinery also lets one fairly compare different bootstrapping algorithms: a noisier bootstrap simply shows up as a higher input noise for the next AP.

Why large precision needs many ciphertexts: TFHE PBS vs Tree-PBS

Using the framework to price operators yields the paper's central practical message (Section 3.3). The plain TFHE PBS in $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ is the best tool for 1 to 5 bit messages, but its cost is essentially exponential: above 4 to 5 bits each extra bit of precision roughly doubles the cost (the polynomial size $N$ must double), and beyond $\approx 11$ bits no correct parameters exist at all. For $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ the noise bound (Remark 4) is

$$t(p,1)=\frac{q}{2^{1+1}\cdot p\cdot z^{*}(p_{\mathrm{fail}})}$$

Equation 10. The alternative for larger integers is to split the message across blocks and use the Tree-PBS of Guimaraes, Borin and Aranha ($\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{GBA21})}$), whose first layer uses the multi-value PBS of [CIM19] and whose later layers use PBS over encrypted LUTs [CGGI20]. Because a Tree-PBS with $i$ blocks involves several PBSs, its noise bound is computed from the per-AP PBS count

$$\eta_i = i\cdot\frac{p^{\,i-1}-1}{p-1} + 1$$

Equation 11 gives the number of PBSs over which the global failure probability must be shared, each PBS being allotted $p_i' = 1-(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}})^{1/\eta_i}$ and then a per-PBS noise bound $t(p,1)=\frac{q}{2^{1+1}\cdot p\cdot z^{*}(p_i')}$. Tree-PBS reaches higher precision (feasible up to 16 bits with 2 blocks, 21 bits with 3 blocks; cheapest of the three only from about 10 bits) but its cost explodes with the number of blocks: a generic 21-bit LUT via Tree-PBS costs more than $2^{20}\times$ a Boolean PBS. The takeaway: above $\approx 9$ bits even Tree-PBS is too expensive, motivating a better generic-LUT algorithm. (The Tree-PBS parameter search has no shortcuts implemented and takes about 26 minutes per curve, versus under a second for the others.)

Figure 1: cost (log scale) versus message precision for the single-ciphertext TFHE PBS ($\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$, blue dots) against the Tree-PBS with 2 and 3 blocks ($\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{GBA21})}$, red plus and green triangles), at four dot-product 2-norms $\log_2(\nu)\in\{4,6,8,10\}$, with $q=2^{64}$, $p_{\mathrm{fail}}\approx 2^{-35}$ and one padding bit. The single-ciphertext curve stops near 11 bits; splitting into blocks reaches further but grows fast.

Encoding large integers: carry buffers, radix, CRT, and the hybrid

To compute on large integers, a message is decomposed into several small LWE ciphertexts. The authors first refine the single-ciphertext layout (Section 2.2): the plaintext space is split into a message subspace (an integer mod $\beta$, the base), a carry subspace (room to absorb carries from leveled additions and multiplications), and a bit of padding (or more) for bootstrapping; together the message-plus-carry modulus is written $p$. A metadata called the degree of fullness,

$$\deg(\mathrm{ct})=\frac{\mu}{p-1}\in\mathbb{Q}, \qquad 0\le m\le\mu\lt p,$$

Equation 12, with $\mu$ the known worst-case message value, tracks how full the carry buffer is. While $\deg(\mathrm{ct})$ stays below 1, additions and scalar multiplications proceed without a bootstrap; a PBS is triggered only to flush carries and reduce noise (Remark 1). Supported leveled operators include addition, multiplication by a small constant, opposite (with a correction term), subtraction, univariate function evaluation (PBS), bi/multi-variate function evaluation (concatenate two messages by rescaling the first to $\mu_2+1$ and adding, then evaluate one PBS on the concatenation [CZB+22]), full ciphertext-ciphertext multiplication, and carry/message extraction. The authors also handle a PBS with $p$ not a power of two by carefully sizing the redundant mega-cases of the LUT polynomial:

$$\widetilde{L} = X^{-\lfloor \frac{N}{2p}\rceil}\cdot\left(\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\left\lfloor \frac{q}{p'}\cdot y_{\lfloor i\cdot p/N\rfloor}\right\rceil\cdot X^i\right)$$

Equation 13 is the redundant LUT polynomial for $L:\mathbb{Z}_p\to\mathbb{Z}_{p'},\ x\mapsto y_x$; with $p$ not a power of two the mega-cases take size $\lfloor N/p\rfloor$ or $\lceil N/p\rceil$, and the failure analysis uses the worst case $\lfloor N/p\rfloor$ (the probability of landing in the wrong mega-case being bounded by the tail of $e_{\mathrm{MS}}$, the error after modulus switching).

Two classical multi-ciphertext schemes are then generalized:

  • Radix: a number mod $\Omega=\prod_{i=0}^{\kappa-1}\beta_i$ stored as digits in mixed bases, like ordinary base-10 notation, with $\mathrm{msg}=m_0+\sum_{i=1}^{\kappa-1}m_i\cdot\prod_{j=0}^{i-1}\beta_j$. Cheap schoolbook arithmetic and carry propagation, but historically limited to $\Omega$ that factor into small bases (no arbitrary, e.g. prime, modulus). Good practice keeps $p_i\le 2^8$.
  • CRT: residues $x_i = x \bmod \omega_i$ for co-prime $\omega_i$, each in its own ciphertext, giving independent parallel arithmetic, but limited by the scarcity of small ($\lt 8$-bit) co-primes. A native CRT variant drops the padding bit and carry buffer (encoding $m_i$ as $\lceil \frac{q}{\beta_i}\cdot m_i\rceil$), making additions and scalar multiplications essentially free LWE operations, at the price of needing a WoP-PBS for any LUT.

The new hybrid representation (Figure 2) puts a CRT layer on top - co-prime $\Omega_0,\dots,\Omega_{\kappa-1}$ with $\Omega=\prod_i\Omega_i$ and $\mathrm{msg}_i=\mathrm{msg}\bmod\Omega_i$ - and a radix layer beneath each residue (each $\Omega_i$ has its own bases $(\beta_{i,\kappa_i-1},\dots,\beta_{i,0})$). It recovers both the CRT's parallelism and the radix's ability to represent any modulus, and strictly generalizes pure-CRT (all $\kappa_i=1$) and pure-radix ($\kappa=1$). The paper also contributes two new algorithms for homomorphic modular reduction for any modulus in the radix approach, closing a previously open gap.

Figure 2: the hybrid encoding. A message $\mathrm{msg}\bmod\Omega$ is first split by CRT into residues $\mathrm{msg}_i=\mathrm{msg}\bmod\Omega_i$, and each residue is then represented in radix with its own bases $(\beta_{i,\kappa_i-1},\dots,\beta_{i,0})$.

Two new modular-reduction algorithms for radix

Generalizing radix to any $\Omega$ with $\prod_{j=0}^{\kappa-2}\beta_j \lt \Omega \lt \prod_{j=0}^{\kappa-1}\beta_j$ only requires a homomorphic modular reduction. The authors give two complementary methods (full listings in the Algorithms appendix).

The first method (Algorithm 1, ModReduction1) reduces the most significant block - which carries $m_{\kappa-1}\cdot\prod_{i=0}^{\kappa-2}\beta_i$ and may exceed $\Omega$ - by evaluating $\kappa$ LUTs on it. Each LUT $P_j$ extracts one digit $x'_j$ of $\big(x\cdot\prod_{h=0}^{\kappa-2}\beta_h\bmod\Omega\big)$ in the base-$(\beta_0,\dots,\beta_{\kappa-1})$ decomposition (a function $\mathbb{Z}_{p_{\kappa-1}}\to\mathbb{Z}_{\beta_j}$), and the lower digits are added back into the lower blocks while block $\kappa-1$ is replaced by digit $\kappa-1$:

Algorithm 1: ModReduction1((ct_0, ..., ct_{k-1}), PUB)
  Context: P_j is the r-redundant LUT for
      Z_{p_{k-1}} -> Z_{beta_j},  x |-> x'_j = Decomp_j( x * prod_{h=0}^{k-2} beta_h  mod Omega )
  Input:   (ct_0, ..., ct_{k-1}) encrypting msg = m_0 + sum_{i=1}^{k-1} m_i * prod_{j=0}^{i-1} beta_j
           PUB: public material for KS-PBS
  Output:  (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}) encrypting msg mod Omega

  // Decompose block k-1 with respect to base (beta_0, ..., beta_{k-1})
  1  for j in [0 .. k-1]:
  2      c_j <- KS-PBS(ct_{k-1}, PUB, P_j)
  // Add the decomposition into all blocks up to k-2
  3  for j in [0 .. k-2]:
  4      ct'_j <- Add(ct_j, c_j)
  // Replace block k-1 by the (k-1)-th element of the decomposition
  5  ct'_{k-1} <- c_{k-1}
  6  return (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1})

The $\kappa$ KS-PBSs in line 2 can be batched with PBSmanyLUT [CLOT21] or multi-value bootstrapping [CIM19].

The second method (Algorithm 2, ModReduction2) exploits the radix decomposition of the scaling factor of the MSB block reduced mod $\Omega$:

$$\prod_{h=0}^{\kappa-2}\beta_h \bmod\Omega = \nu_0 + \nu_1\beta_0 + \nu_2\beta_0\beta_1 + \dots + \nu_{\kappa-2}\prod_{j=0}^{\kappa-3}\beta_j$$

Equation 14. If $\nu_{\kappa-1}=0$ and the other $\nu_j$ are small (ideally many zero), it just multiplies the MSB block by the constants $\nu_j$ and subtracts (or adds, for negative $\nu_j$) from the lower blocks. For a 3-block integer $m=m_0+m_1\beta_0+m_2\beta_0\beta_1$ with $\beta_0\beta_1=\nu_0+\nu_1\beta_0+\nu_2\beta_0\beta_1\bmod\Omega$,

$$m \equiv (m_0 + m_2\nu_0) + (m_1 + m_2\nu_1)\beta_0 + (m_2\nu_2)\beta_0\beta_1 \pmod{\Omega}$$

Equation 15. If $\nu_2=0$ the last block is emptied. The paper's worked example takes $\Omega=1055$, $\kappa=3$, $\beta=25$, $p=27$: since $25^2\bmod 1055 = -31 = -1\cdot 25 + 1$ we have $\nu_0=1,\nu_1=-1,\nu_2=0$, so block 0 gets $+1\times$ block 2, block 1 gets $-1\times$ block 2, and block 2 becomes an encryption of 0. This second method needs only constant multiplications and subtractions (plus a carry propagation if a buffer fills, and a homomorphic Decomp step if the bases differ), so it is cheaper than the first whenever the $\nu_j$ are small.

The new WoP-PBS: a padding-free generic LUT over many ciphertexts

The headline algorithmic contribution (Section 4.2.1, Algorithm 3) is a WoP-PBS: a programmable bootstrap that needs no bit of padding, accepts one or several input LWE ciphertexts, can round/truncate each input to a chosen precision, and can evaluate several LUTs on the same inputs for roughly the price of one. It is built from two TFHE primitives, circuit bootstrapping and (mixed/vertical/horizontal) packing [CGGI20], in three stages (full listing in the Algorithms appendix):

Algorithm 3 (structure): WoP-PBS over kappa input blocks
  Input:  (ct_0, ..., ct_{kappa-1}); LUT L = [l_0, ..., l_{Omega-1}]; public keys PUB
  // Stage 1 -- Bit Extract (lines 1..12): peel every message bit into its own LWE
  for i in [0 .. kappa-1]:
      for j in [0 .. delta_i - 1]:
          shift ct_i so the next bit reaches the MSB (multiply by 2^{delta_i-1-i})
          add correction term epsilon_i                  // avoids sign errors near 0 and q/2
          c_i <- KS-PBS(shifted ct_i, scaled negacyclic sign LUT)  // generalized PBS reads next bit
          subtract the extracted bit from ct_i            // clears it; chain LSB -> MSB
          C_{i,j} <- CircuitBootstrap(c_i)                 // Stage 2: bit-LWE -> GGSW (dominant cost)
  // Stage 3 -- Vertical packing (VPLut, line 13): GGSW bits drive a CMux tree
  out <- VPLut({C_{i,j}}, L)                              // CMux tree + blind rotation + sample extract
  return out                                              // ( LWE(l_0(m)), ..., LWE(l_{kappa-1}(m)) )

The bit-extraction correction term avoids a wrong sign when an encoded value sits near $0$ or $q/2$. For the first bit of a block with non-power-of-two base $\beta_i=\beta_i'\cdot 2^k$ (native CRT case), it is derived as

$$\epsilon_i = \left\lfloor \frac{q}{4\beta_i'}\right\rfloor - 2^{\alpha_i-3} = \left\lfloor \frac{q\cdot 2^{k-2}}{\beta_i}\right\rfloor - 2^{\alpha_i-3}, \qquad \alpha_i=\lceil\log_2\beta_i\rceil$$

Equation 16; the $2^{\alpha_i-3}$ term is negligible relative to $q$ and is dropped, so in practice $\epsilon_i=\lfloor q\cdot 2^{k-2}/\beta_i\rfloor$ (and simply $\epsilon=q/4$ for power-of-two bases and for all subsequent bits, since the next bit is $0$ after the shift). Circuit bootstrapping is the dominant cost - each one is a PBS followed by several private functional key-switches - so the algorithm scales linearly in the number of message bits until, for very large inputs (about 28 bits with their parameters), the mixed-packing stage overtakes it. The paper gives closed-form variance formulae for the circuit bootstrap $\mathrm{Var}(E_{\mathrm{CB}})$ (summing PBS and private functional KS terms from the [CLOT21] noise models) and for the full WoP-PBS,

$$\mathrm{Var}(E_{\mathrm{WoP\text{-}PBS}}) = \mathrm{Var}(E_{\mathrm{CB}}) + \left(\sum_{i=0}^{\kappa-1}\delta_i\right)\cdot(\text{mixed-packing terms})$$

Equation 17, where $\delta_i$ is the number of bits extracted from block $i$. Several optimizations are noted: chaining the bit-extraction PBSs from LSB to MSB; replacing the packing key-switches with an initial LWE-to-GLWE KS plus an external product (after [CCR19]) to shrink keys; using a generalized PBS so the modulus switch directly reads the next bit; and using PBSmanyLUT to do all PBSs of a circuit bootstrap at once when $\vartheta\gt 0$. For special LUTs such as ReLU or the radix-mode sign function only the MSBs matter, so packing collapses and the cost becomes linear in the number of blocks (Remark 6). Public material is also far smaller than Tree-PBS: for 18 bits of precision a Tree-PBS key is 1.65 GB versus 0.926 GB for the new WoP-PBS (Remark 9). (Remark 8 notes that for inputs beyond about 30 bits the LUT itself becomes too large even in cleartext - e.g. a 30-bit LUT is 8 GB - so neither technique is practical.)

Figure 3: cleartext view of the new WoP-PBS on a toy 16-bit message split into blocks $m_0,\dots,m_3$. Each block is bit-extracted and circuit-bootstrapped into GGSW bits, which then drive a CMux tree (vertical packing) plus a final select-in-LUT (blind rotation) to produce $L[m]$.

Comparing the operators: where each bootstrap wins

With all three operators priced by the same framework (Section 4.2.3, held to the same $p_{\mathrm{fail}}\approx 2^{-35}$ and 2-norm), the comparison becomes apples-to-apples. The new WoP-PBS $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$ (in 1/2/4-block variants) shares its failure budget over the $\kappa$ input ciphertexts via $p_i' = 1-(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}})^{1/\kappa}$ and uses the noise bound

$$t(p,0)=\frac{q}{2^{1}\cdot p\cdot z^{*}(p_i')}$$

Equation 18 (note: no padding bit, hence the $2^1$ rather than $2^2$, and Remark 7 justifies counting only the first, dominant, bit-extraction PBS per block for $\nu\ge 4$ and $p_{\mathrm{fail}}\le 2^{-25}$). Between 1 and 9 bits $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ is more efficient, but from 10 bits and above $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$ wins and keeps finding solutions where the classical PBS cannot - up to 24 bits. At 18 bits the 2-block variant is $\approx 2^{7}\times$ faster than the 3-block Tree-PBS, and a 24-bit LUT costs only $\approx 2^{10}\times$ a 1-bit classical PBS.

Figure 4: cost (log scale) versus precision comparing $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ (single-ciphertext PBS, blue dots), $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{GBA21})}$ (Tree-PBS, 2/3 blocks, red/green), and the new $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$ WoP-PBS in 1/2/4-block variants (brown diamonds, pink plus, pink squares), at four 2-norms. The WoP-PBS curves rise much more gently with precision than Tree-PBS and reach 24 bits.

The same framework also compares the new WoP-PBS against the competing padding-free bootstrap of [LMP21] (Section 4.2.4), which - using two [CGGI20] PBSs as subroutines, each allotted $p_i'=1-(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}})^{1/2}$ - finds no feasible solution above $\approx 10$ bits, just like $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$. The practical division of labour that emerges: classical PBS for 1 to 5 bits, and the WoP-PBS (over multi-ciphertext integers) for everything larger, with Tree-PBS rarely the best choice.

Figure 5: cost (log scale) versus precision comparing the new WoP-PBS $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$ (blue dots) against the padding-free bootstrap of $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{LMP21})}$ (red plus), at four 2-norms. [LMP21] does not scale past about 10 bits, exhibiting the same two-part exponential blow-up as the classical PBS.

Concrete parameters and benchmarks (TFHE-rs / Concrete)

All benchmarks (Section 4.3) run on TFHE-rs, the Rust successor to Zama's Concrete library [CJL+20], on a single Intel Xeon Platinum 8375C at 2.90 GHz (504 GB RAM, though a laptop suffices), with all parameters produced by the optimization framework, 128-bit security, and (by default) $p_{\mathrm{fail}}\le 2^{-13.9}$ (standard score 4). Table 1 gives seven $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ sets (each with a padding bit, a message modulus $p$ and a 2-norm $\nu$, reporting $n$ and its noise, $(k,\log_2 N)$ and its noise, and base/level for the KS and PBS): e.g. set #1 ($p=2^2$, $\nu=3$) uses $n=615,\,k=4,\,N=2^9$; set #6 ($p=2^8$, $\nu=17$) uses $n=983,\,k=1,\,N=2^{14}$. Table 2 gives five $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$ sets (#8 to #12, no padding bit) specifying how many bits to extract per block and the number $\kappa$ of input ciphertexts, plus base/level for the LWE-to-LWE KS, PBS, packing KS and circuit bootstrap; sets #11/#12 encode the native-CRT bases. The biggest 2-norm a block can ever face is

$$\nu_{\max} = \left\lfloor\frac{p-1}{\beta-1}\right\rfloor$$

Equation 19 (Remark 11), since a fresh encoding is at worst $\beta-1$ and the biggest value before flushing carries is $p-1$. The framework can also generate compatible pairs of parameter sets (matching $k\cdot N$ and absorbing each other's output noise) so a circuit switches freely between classical-PBS arithmetic and WoP-PBS LUTs; this works for couples (#1,#8) and (#2,#9) by optimizing $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$ first, but (#3,#10) needs the order reversed.

Representative timings:

  • 16-bit radix ($\Omega=2^{16}$, Table 3): addition $\approx 6.67$ to $12.8\,\mu\text{s}$ (no PBS, denoted $+^{*}$), multiplication $\approx 5.73$ to $29.0\,\text{s}$, carry cleaning $\approx 657$ to $932\,\text{ms}$, generic LUT via WoP-PBS $\approx 823\,\text{ms}$ to $1.80\,\text{s}$.
  • 16-bit CRT ($\Omega=7\cdot 8\cdot 9\cdot 11\cdot 13\approx 2^{16}$, Table 4): multiply $\approx 401\,\text{ms}$ sequential ($\approx 80.3\,\text{ms}$ on 5 threads); a CRT-friendly polynomial-function LUT is as cheap as a per-residue carry cleaning. A generic (non-CRT-friendly) LUT is much slower ($\approx 23.1\,\text{s}$ sequential, $4.61\,\text{s}$ on 5 threads) here because the parameters were tuned for $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ compatibility.
  • 16-bit native CRT ($\Omega=7\cdot 8\cdot 9\cdot 11\cdot 13$, Table 5): multiply $\approx 7.42\,\text{s}$, LUT $\approx 3.81\,\text{s}$ sequential ($\approx 1.65\,\text{s}$ and $0.761\,\text{s}$ on 5 threads), additions essentially free ($\approx 4.32\,\mu\text{s}$).
  • 32-bit: radix multiply $\approx 9.60$ to $43.8\,\text{s}$ depending on block count; hybrid (CRT over $\Omega=2^5\cdot 3^5\cdot 5^4\cdot 7^4$) multiply $\approx 5.17\,\text{s}$ sequential / $1.82\,\text{s}$ on 4 threads; native CRT ($\Omega=3\cdot 11\cdot 13\cdot 19\cdot 23\cdot 29\cdot 31\cdot 32$) multiply $\approx 36.8\,\text{s}$ sequential / $5.31\,\text{s}$ on 8 threads. Generic LUT on 32-bit integers is impractical. Remark 12 cautions that with 32 single-bit blocks the number of APs in a multiply is quadratic in block count, inflating the overall failure probability - another reason fewer, larger blocks (hybrid) win at 32 bits.

The parameter search itself is fast: most curves are generated in $\lt 1\,\text{s}$; only Tree-PBS (no shortcuts implemented) takes $\approx 26\,\text{min}$ (Remark 13).

The full framework: whole-graph failure, PBS insertion, key positions

Section 5 lifts the per-AP analysis to a full-fledged framework: the input is a crypto-free plain DAG (Definition 13: additions, multiplications, subtractions, LUTs, each edge labelled by precision and public/private), and the output is simultaneously the best FHE DAG and its parameter set, chosen over a space $\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{FHE}}(\mathcal{G})$ of translation rules (e.g. a multiply becomes two PBSs [CJL+20] or a leveled multiply [CLOT21]; a LUT becomes a KS-PBS for 1 to 8 bits or the WoP-PBS for more):

$$(\widehat{\mathcal{G}},\widehat{x}) = \arg\min_{\mathcal{G},x}\ \mathrm{Cost}(\mathcal{G},x) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad \mathcal{G}\in\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{FHE}}(\mathcal{G}),\ \ x\in\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G})$$

Equation 20 is the joint graph-and-parameter optimization. Several features sharpen the result:

  • Whole-graph failure probability (Section 5.2): individual-AP failure probabilities combine as an upper bound

$$p_{\mathrm{fail}}(\mathcal{G}) \le 1-\prod_{i\in I}\bigl(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}}(A_i)\bigr)$$

Equation 21. Using domination, the dominant AP is allotted $p_{\mathrm{fail}}(A_{\mathrm{dom}})=1-(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}}(\mathcal{G}))^{1/|I'|}$, and a dominated AP $A_1$ (with $\nu_1\le\nu_2$, $t_1\ge t_2$) gets a tighter standard score $\kappa_1$, derived from $\sigma_i^2=\sigma_{\mathrm{BR}}^2\nu_i^2+\sigma_{\mathrm{KS}}^2+\sigma_{\mathrm{MS}}^2$ and $\sigma_1^2\le t_2^2-(\nu_2^2-\nu_1^2)D$:

$$\kappa_1 = \frac{\Delta_1}{2}\cdot\left(D\cdot(\nu_1^2-\nu_2^2) + \Bigl(\frac{\Delta_2}{2\kappa}\Bigr)^2\right)^{-\frac{1}{2}}, \qquad D=\min_x\sigma_{\mathrm{BR}}^2(x)$$

Equation 22, so that $p_{\mathrm{fail}}(A_1)\le 1-\mathrm{erf}(\kappa_1/\sqrt{2})$. The user thus specifies a single target failure probability for the whole use-case, and a simple loop iteratively rebalances $p_{\mathrm{fail}}(A_{\mathrm{dom}})$ until the realized $\widehat{p}_{\mathrm{fail}}(\mathcal{G})$ is within a tolerance $\delta_{\mathrm{fail}}$.

  • Optimal PBS insertion (Section 5.3, Theorem 2): when a dot product has a large 2-norm it forces large, slow parameters; the framework can automatically split the DP into $d+1$ sub-DPs joined by extra PBSs, trading more bootstraps for smaller, cheaper parameters. The optimal two-way split solves

$$\min_{\omega_{i,1},\Lambda}\ \max(\nu_1,\nu_2) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad \omega_i=\omega_{i,1}\Lambda+\omega_{i,2},\ \ \gcd(\omega_{i,1},\Lambda)=1,\ \ \nu_j=\sqrt{\textstyle\sum_i\omega_{j,1}^2}$$

Equation 23, which balances the two halves' 2-norms ($\nu_1^2\approx\nu_2^2$). If the weights are unknown but uniform in $[-2^p,2^p]$, a trivial radix decomposition of the weights with level $d+1$ and $\log_2\beta=\frac{p+1}{d+1}$ works well.

  • Key-switch position (Section 5.4, Theorem 3): it is analytically always at least as good to place the KS immediately before the PBS (the [CJP21] ordering $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$) as the [CGGI20] gate-bootstrapping ordering $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CGGI20})}$, where the KS noise gets amplified by the DP ($\sigma_{\mathrm{out},2}^2=(\sigma_{\mathrm{in}}^2+\sigma_{\mathrm{KS}}^2)\nu^2+\sigma_{\mathrm{MS}}^2$ versus $\sigma_{\mathrm{out},1}^2=\sigma_{\mathrm{in}}^2\nu^2+\sigma_{\mathrm{KS}}^2+\sigma_{\mathrm{MS}}^2$). The KS-free variant $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{KS-free})}$ is always worst and finds no solution past 7 bits; $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CGGI20})}$ finds none past 8 bits. See Figure 6.

Figure 6: cost (log scale) versus precision (up to 11 bits) comparing $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ (KS before PBS, blue dots), $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CGGI20})}$ (KS after, as in gate bootstrapping, red plus), and $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{KS-free})}$ (no key switch, green triangles), at four 2-norms. $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ is always cheapest; the gap widens with $\nu$, confirming Theorem 3.

  • Failure-probability spectrum (Section 5.5): the cost of tightening $p_{\mathrm{fail}}$ from $2^{-14}$ down to $2^{-50}$ is quantified for both AP types (Figures 7 and 8). For the single-ciphertext PBS, tightening can jump the cost when $N$ must quadruple rather than double; for the WoP-PBS the curves merely shift up while staying near-linear in precision.

Figure 7: cost (log scale) versus precision (1 to 12 bits) for the single-ciphertext PBS $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ at four target failure probabilities $p_{\mathrm{fail}}\in\{2^{-14},2^{-20},2^{-35},2^{-50}\}$ and fixed $\log_2(\nu)=4$. Each extra bit roughly doubles the cost, with jumps where $N$ must grow $4\times$ instead of $2\times$.

Figure 8: the same failure-probability spectrum for the new WoP-PBS $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$. The curves stay near-linear in precision (up to 24 bits) and merely shift upward as $p_{\mathrm{fail}}$ tightens; the $2^{-35}$ and $2^{-50}$ curves nearly overlap.

  • Multiple public keys (Section 5.6, Theorems 4 to 5): allowing several bootstrap/key-switch keys lets high-noise-budget parts of a graph use smaller, faster keys. Sorting keys by added noise, the optimal key assignment $\vec{\delta}=(\delta_0,\dots,\delta_{Y-1})$ along increasing 2-norm satisfies $\delta_i\ge\delta_{i+1}$, which prunes the assignment search from size $X^Y$.
  • Consensus-friendly TFHE (Section 5.7): the FFT is non-deterministic across implementations (same message, different LSBs), so an atomic pattern is made bit-reproducible by appending a rounding step after the PBS that discards the differing LSBs, with an extra feasible set $\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{other}}(\mathcal{G})=\{x\mid\forall i,\ E_{\mathrm{PBS}_i}(x)=0\}$ and the constraint $\frac{q}{2\beta_{\mathrm{PBS}}^{\ell_{\mathrm{PBS}}}}\gt \mathrm{error}_{\mathrm{FFT}}(x)$. This is useful for blockchain settings where all miners must agree on the exact ciphertext.

Relation to TFHE and takeaways

This work sits squarely on top of the TFHE line [CGGI20] and its programmable bootstrap, and on the generalized PBS / first WoP-PBS of [CLOT21]. Its contribution is twofold and complementary: a methodology (the optimization framework) that finally makes choosing secure, correct and fast parameters an automatable problem for bootstrapping-heavy schemes, and a set of large-precision constructions (hybrid radix/CRT integers, arbitrary-modulus modular reduction, and the padding-free multi-ciphertext WoP-PBS) that make 16-bit and 32-bit homomorphic integer arithmetic and generic LUTs practical.

Practically, the results are realized in TFHE-rs, the successor to Zama's Concrete library, and the framework is the engine behind Concrete's automatic parameter selection, so the paper is effectively the design document for how Zama's compiler turns a user's plain program into a secure, correct, fast FHE circuit. Recurring lessons: for large precision, prefer several small ciphertexts over one big one; the classical TFHE PBS is the right tool only up to $\approx 5$ bits; the KS should sit right before the PBS; and a faithful, FFT-corrected noise model plus the lattice-estimator are what let the optimizer trust its own answers. Stated future work (Section 6) includes optimizing the integer representation itself (moduli, block counts), optimizing the graph topology, and extending the framework to non-TFHE schemes.

Algorithms

The paper's formal algorithms are collected here as self-contained at-a-glance listings (the body's Algorithm 3 sketch is fully spelled out below). Algorithms 1 and 2 perform homomorphic modular reduction for radix integers; Algorithm 3 is the new WoP-PBS; Algorithm 4 (Decomp) and Algorithm 5 (Mult) are the radix decomposition and multiplication used as subroutines. Notation: $\mathrm{KS\text{-}PBS}(\cdot)$ is a key-switch followed by a generalized PBS; $\Delta_i$ is a scaling factor; $\delta_i$ is the number of message bits in $\mathrm{ct}_i$; $\kappa,\vartheta$ parameterize the generalized PBS modulus switch.

Algorithm 1: (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}) <- ModReduction1((ct_0, ..., ct_{k-1}), PUB)
  Context:
     P_j : r-redundant LUT for  Z_{p_{k-1}} -> Z_{beta_j},
           x |-> x'_j = Decomp_j( x * prod_{h=0}^{k-2} beta_h  mod Omega ),
           the j-th digit in base (beta_0, ..., beta_{k-1}),
           so  x * prod_{h=0}^{k-2} beta_h mod Omega = x'_0 + sum_{i=1}^{k-1} x'_i * prod_{j=0}^{i-1} beta_j
  Input:
     (ct_0, ..., ct_{k-1}) encrypting msg = m_0 + sum_{i=1}^{k-1} m_i * prod_{j=0}^{i-1} beta_j,
           ct_i encrypts m_i with parameters (beta_i, p_i)
     PUB : public material for KS-PBS
  Output:
     (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}) encrypting msg mod Omega

  1  for j in [0 .. k-1]:                       // decompose block k-1 into base (beta_0,...,beta_{k-1})
  2      c_j <- KS-PBS(ct_{k-1}, PUB, P_j)
  3  for j in [0 .. k-2]:                        // add decomposition to all blocks up to k-2
  4      ct'_j <- Add(ct_j, c_j)
  5  ct'_{k-1} <- c_{k-1}                          // replace block k-1 by the (k-1)-th digit
  6  return (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1})
Algorithm 2: (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}) <- ModReduction2((ct_0, ..., ct_{k-1}), PUB)
  Context:
     nu = (nu_0, ..., nu_{k-1}) a decomposition s.t.
        prod_{h=0}^{k-2} beta_h mod Omega = nu_0 + nu_1*beta_0 + nu_2*beta_0*beta_1 + ...
  Input:
     (ct_0, ..., ct_{k-1}) encrypting msg (ct_i has parameters (beta_i, p_i))
  Output:
     (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}) encrypting msg mod Omega

  1  (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}) <- (ct_0, ..., ct_{k-2}, 0)   // copy input, zero the MSB block
  2  for j in [0 .. k-2]:
  3      if nu_j < 0:                                       // multiply MSB block by |nu_j| (positive const)
  4          c_j <- ScalarMul(ct_{k-1}, -nu_j)
  5      else:
  6          c_j <- ScalarMul(ct_{k-1}, nu_j)
  7      (c_{j,0}, ..., c_{j,k-j-1}) <- Decomp(c_j, (beta_i)_{i in [j,k-1]}, (p_i)_{i in [j,k-1]}, PUB)
  8      (c'_{j,0}, ..., c'_{j,k-1}) <- Pad((c_{j,0}, ..., c_{j,k-j-1}), j, (beta_i), (p_i))
  9      if nu_j < 0:
 10          (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}) <- Add((ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}), (c'_{j,0}, ..., c'_{j,k-1}))
 11      else:
 12          (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}) <- Sub((ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}), (c'_{j,0}, ..., c'_{j,k-1}))
 13  return (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1})
Algorithm 3: ct_out <- WoP-PBS((ct_0, ..., ct_{kappa-1}), PUB, L)
  Context:
     Delta_i        : scaling factor for ciphertext ct_i
     delta_i        : message bits in ct_i starting from Delta_i;  Omega = 2^{sum_i delta_i}
     (beta_CB, l_CB): base and level of the output GGSW of circuit bootstrapping
     (kappa, theta) : generalized-PBS modulus-switch parameters [CLOT21]
  Input:
     (ct_0, ..., ct_{kappa-1}) encrypting msg = (m_0, ..., m_{kappa-1})
     PUB : all public keys;   L = [l_0, ..., l_{Omega-1}], l_h in Z_omega
  Output:
     ct_out encrypting l_msg

  1  for i in [0 .. kappa-1]:
  2      for j in [0 .. delta_i - 2]:            // ---- Bit Extract (LSB -> MSB) ----
  3          eps_i = q/4
  4          if j == 0 and q/p_i not integer:    // native-CRT / non-power-of-two base correction
  5              eps_i <- floor( q * 2^{k-2} / beta_i )
  6          alpha_{i,j} = Delta_i * 2^j / 2
  7          L_{i,j} = [ -alpha_{i,j}, ..., -alpha_{i,j} ]      // scaled negacyclic sign LUT
  8          c_i <- KS-PBS( ct_i * 2^{delta_i-1-i} + (0,...,0,eps_i), PUB, L_{i,j},
                          (kappa = log2(Delta_i)+j, theta = 0) )
  9          c'_i <- c_i + (0, ..., 0, alpha_{i,j})
 10          ct_i <- Sub(ct_i, c'_i)              // clear the extracted bit
 11          C_{i,j} <- CircuitBootstrap(c'_i, PUB, (beta_CB, l_CB),
                          (kappa = log2(Delta_i)+j, theta = 0))   // bit-LWE -> GGSW
 12      C_{i,delta_i-1} <- CircuitBootstrap(ct_i, PUB, (beta_CB, l_CB),
                          (kappa = log2(Delta_i)+delta_i-1, theta = 0))   // last bit
 13  ct_out <- VPLut( { C_{i,j} }_{i in [0,kappa-1], j in [0,delta_i-1]}, L )   // vertical packing
 14  return ct_out
Algorithm 4: (ct_j)_{j in [0,gamma]} <- Decomp(ct_in, beta_vec, p_vec, PUB)
  Context:
     (q, p, deg) : parameters of ct_in;   mu = deg*(p-1);   gamma = gamma_{beta_vec}(mu)
     P_{i,beta_vec} : a LUT for  x -> r_{i,beta_vec}(x) * q / (2*p_i),  i in [0, kappa-1]
  Input:
     ct_in : LWE encryption of m;   (p_vec, beta_vec) in N^{kappa} x N^{kappa};   PUB
  Output:
     (ct_j)_{j in [0,gamma]} encrypting m

  1  for j in [0 .. gamma]:
  2      ct_j <- KS-PBS(ct_in, PUB, P_j)
  3          // ct_j is an LWE encryption with parameters
  4          //   ( q, beta_j, p_j, deg = min( (beta_j - 1)/(p_j - 1), q_{j-1,beta_vec}(mu)/(p_j-1) ) )
  5  return (ct_j)_{j in [0,gamma]}
Algorithm 5: (ct^out_0, ..., ct^out_{k-1}) <- Mult( (ct^1_0,...,ct^1_{k1-1}), (ct^2_0,...,ct^2_{k2-1}), PUB )
  Context:
     (q, p_{j,i}, beta_{j,i}, deg_{j,i}) : parameters of ct^j_i
     mu_{j,i} = deg_{j,i}*(p_{j,i}-1) + 1,  j in {1,2}
     LUTs P encode  x -> r(.) * ( (x mod mu_{2,i}) * floor(x / mu_{2,i}) ) * q / (2*p)
  Input:
     (ct^i_0, ..., ct^i_{ki-1}) encrypting msg_i under s,  i in {1,2};   PUB
  Output:
     (ct^out_0, ..., ct^out_{k-1}) encrypting msg_1 * msg_2 under s

  1  for i in [0 .. k1-1]:
  2      ct_tmp <- LweBasisChange(ct^1_i, p_{2,0}, beta_{2,0}, PUB)      // put block in the right basis
  3      (ct^tmp_0, ..., ct^tmp_{k-1}) <- OneBlockMul(ct_tmp, (ct^2_0,...,ct^2_{k2-1}), PUB)
  4      (ct^out_0, ..., ct^out_{k-1}) <- Add((ct^out_0,...,ct^out_{k-1}), (ct^tmp_0,...,ct^tmp_{k-1}))
  5  return (ct^out_0, ..., ct^out_{k-1})

Two further procedures referenced above are described in prose rather than as numbered algorithms in the source. The multi-radix Tree-PBS (Supplementary Material C.1, generalizing [GBA21] to mixed bases) evaluates a LUT $L=[l_0,\dots,l_{\Omega-1}]$ over a radix integer as follows:

Tree-PBS((ct_0, ..., ct_{kappa-1}), PUB, L):
  1  B <- { beta_i : i in [0, kappa-1] };  theta(beta_i) = the block m_i associated to beta_i
  2  while B is not empty:
  3      beta_max <- max(B)
  4      split L into  nu = (prod_{beta in B} beta) / beta_max  smaller LUTs (L_0, ..., L_{nu-1}),
            each holding beta_max elements of L
  5      for each L_t:  compute a PBS using the block theta(beta_max) as selector
  6      pack the nu PBS results (via key switching) into a new, smaller LUT L
  7      B <- B - { beta_max }
  8  return ct_out      // a single LWE ciphertext encrypting L[msg]

The carry / message extraction primitives each run one KS-PBS on the same input with a different LUT: $P_{\text{carry-ext}}$ realizes $x\mapsto\lfloor x/\beta\rfloor$ (output degree $\lfloor\mu_1/\beta\rfloor$) and $P_{\text{msg-ext}}$ realizes $x\mapsto x\bmod\beta$ (output degree $\min(\frac{\beta-1}{p-1},\deg_1)$); more than one PBS may be needed if the carry subspace is larger than the message subspace.
